items.reality: Faster algorithms often cost more memory; memory has its own cost (RAM, GC, eviction). Optimize the *scarce* resource, not whichever number you can lower most.
items.example:
```python
# Brute KNN is slow but uses no extra memory. HNSW is fast but holds GB of index.
# Pick by what you can afford.
```

items.reality: Only up to a point. Above a threshold, extra memory only adds GC pressure or page-cache churn. There's a knee in the curve; profile to find it.
items.example:
```python
# Doubling lru_cache size past the working set yields ~0 hit-rate improvement.
```

items.reality: Without overlapping sub-problems the cache never hits — you pay the memory and add complexity for no speedup. And without proper invalidation, cached values go stale.
items.example:
```python
# Mergesort sub-problems don't overlap; memoization adds zero value.
```

items.reality: Quantization saves memory and *often* speeds up inference, but it costs quality. The tradeoff is across three axes — time, space, and accuracy — not two.
items.example:
```python
# int4 weights: ~4× smaller, often 2-3× faster, with measurable quality drops on hard prompts.
```
